region_of_origin: Northern China (Inner Mongolia / Xinjiang culinary tradition)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for scallion-fried lamb (葱爆羊肉). FOODON_03302051 (lamb dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (lamb and mutton meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for the scallion stir-fry preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for scallion-fried lamb as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of scallion-fried lamb is approximately 250 g, comprising thinly sliced cooked lamb with scallions and sauce as served at a Northern Chinese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, composite cuts, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with scallion and sauce component estimates from USDA Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for 葱爆羊肉 (scallion-fried lamb) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb composite cuts (NDB 17003 / FDC ID 174382), adjusted for the stir-fry preparation with scallions, soy sauce, and sesame oil. Estimated dish composition: ~70% cooked lamb, ~15% scallions, ~15% sauce/oil. Energy and macros recalculated accordingly. Sodium elevated to reflect soy sauce contribution.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on cooked lamb and low-carbohydrate stir-fry dishes
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 葱爆羊肉. GI estimated at 30 based on the analogous category of high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked meat dishes with minimal starchy components. Cooked lamb and similar meat-dominant stir-fries consistently fall in the GI 25–35 range in the Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (30 × 3.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 2.85, rounded to 2.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce (~480 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced soy sauce, as a 250 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, approaching 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.8 g/100 g) and low glycemic index (~30)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Scallion-fried lamb is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions, as its low carbohydrate content and low GI (~30) produce minimal postprandial glucose excursion; however, the sodium content warrants attention in those with comorbid hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.9 g/100 g) and sodium (~480 mg/100 g) from lamb fat and soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in modest portions (≤150 g), as the saturated fat from lamb and sodium from soy sauce both contribute to cardiovascular risk when consumed in excess.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Scallion-fried lamb (葱爆羊肉, cōng bào yángròu) is a classic Northern Chinese stir-fry dish originating from the Inner Mongolian and Xinjiang culinary traditions, widely popular across China and served at Northern Chinese restaurants in Singapore such as Bei Jiang Restaurant. The dish is prepared by flash-frying thinly sliced lamb over very high heat in a wok with generous quantities of scallions (spring onions), garlic, ginger, soy sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, and sesame oil. The cooking technique — known as 'bào' (爆), meaning explosive high-heat frying — produces lamb that is tender and slightly charred at the edges, with a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile and a fragrant aroma from the scallions and sesame oil. The texture is soft and juicy with a slight chew. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-dense (~17.5 g/100 g) and moderate in fat (~12.8 g/100 g), with very low carbohydrate content (~3.8 g/100 g) and a low glycemic index (~30), making it appropriate for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns and suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The primary clinical concern is sodium content (~480 mg/100 g) due to soy sauce, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-containing (soy sauce) and contains sesame allergens. It is halal-compatible when prepared with halal-certified lamb and soy sauce.
common_user_queries: Is cong bao yang rou healthy?
Is scallion lamb good for diabetics?
What does 葱爆羊肉 taste like?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for 葱爆羊肉 (scallion-fried lamb) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked lamb data with component-based adjustment for the stir-fry preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from 'Grains and staples' (input) to 'Meat and alternatives', as 葱爆羊肉 is a lamb-dominant stir-fry dish. 2. Cuisine tags in input included 'Noodle', which is not applicable to this dish; tags not carried forward. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy lamb source entry. 4. Trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) reflects naturally occurring conjugated linoleic acid (CLA) and vaccenic acid present in ruminant lamb fat, not industrially produced trans fat. 5. GI set to 30 as an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in approved sources. 6. Sodium value (~480 mg/100 g) is an estimate reflecting soy sauce contribution in a typical restaurant preparation; actual sodium may vary significantly by recipe. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy lamb composite data and are not independently verified for this specific dish preparation.
